message.content:
You are an INDEPENDENT verifier adjudicating audit finding GitHub issue #10 for /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. You did NOT author it. TRUE_POSITIVE vs FALSE_POSITIVE; try HARD to refute. READ-ONLY: no edits, no git mutation, no gh. You may read files and run `git -C /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay log/show` to inspect, and /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python -m pytest to confirm.

FINDING (#10, stated severity MEDIUM): README.md:10-12 ("Status: documentation phase. The methodology and architecture are being written before any framework code is built.") and docs/CHARTER.md:3 ("Status: Planning / documentation phase.") are stale and contradict the repo, which now contains the full engine scaffold (src/assay_engine, 28 modules), a passing 19-test suite, and CI (commit 2c210f9).

Independently verify: read README.md and CHARTER.md status lines; confirm src/assay_engine exists and tests pass. Decide TRUE/FALSE. Assess MEDIUM severity (docs-accuracy issue).

Return EXACTLY:
VERDICT: TRUE_POSITIVE | FALSE_POSITIVE | PARTIAL
CONFIDENCE: high | medium | low
SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: <...>
REASONING: <your own evidence>
FIX_GUIDANCE: <...>

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by examining the actual state of the repo.

content.content:
1	# hc-assay
2	
3	**A reusable blueprint for rigorous, reproducible empirical ML/NLP on security & privacy data.**
4	
5	`hc-assay` is not a single study. It is an **engine + a methodology** that you clone onto a
6	dataset to build an independent, empirical understanding of it — and, where the dataset
7	carries external expert-asserted claims, to adjudicate those claims against that
8	independent understanding.
9	
10	Status: **documentation phase.** The methodology and architecture are being written before
11	any framework code is built.
12	
13	---
14	
15	## What it does
16	
17	Given a security & privacy dataset, `hc-assay`:
18	
19	1. **Builds an independent empirical baseline** of the data using ML/NLP — deterministic,
20	   versioned, and reproducible by a hostile reviewer. This baseline is the one object in

1	# hc-assay — Charter
2	
3	**Status:** Planning / documentation phase.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Purpose
8	
9	`hc-assay` is a reusable blueprint — an engine plus a methodology — for conducting
10	**rigorous, reproducible empirical machine-learning and NLP analysis of security & privacy

content.content:
commit 2c210f9743f539ffcaca5c4d36eaa8dddb8f7176
Author: Thomas Jones <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Mon Jun 15 18:12:49 2026 -0400

    feat(engine): scaffold dataset-agnostic engine skeleton + methodology core
    
    Stand up the assay_engine package per ARCHITECTURE.md/ADR-0002. The engine
    imports no dataset specifics; adapters implement the contracts it calls.
    
    Contracts (engine <-> adapter boundary):
    - canonical schema (Corpus/Unit/Relation), ingestion-parser Protocol,
      optional external-claims source (quarantined per Firewall A),
      feature-builder Protocol, study definition + two modes.
    
    Methodology core (concrete, enforced in code):
    - three verdicts (supported/contradicted/indeterminate);
    - typed, falsifiable hypotheses with lock/timestamp state;
    - the two firewalls as runtime primitives (ClaimBlindGuard,
      DiscoverConfirmSplit) that raise FirewallViolation, not docstrings;
    - confirmatory machinery (locking gate, held-out + null/permutation+
---CI---
ci.yml

content.text:
All claims in the finding are independently confirmed.

VERDICT: TRUE_POSITIVE
CONFIDENCE: high
SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: MEDIUM is appropriate. This is a docs-accuracy/correctness issue, not a security or functional defect. The stale status lines actively misrepresent the project's maturity to any reader (a "documentation phase, no framework code built" claim while a 28-module engine, contracts/firewalls/methodology core, a passing test suite, and CI all exist). Misleading but non-breaking; MEDIUM is a fair call, arguably could be LOW since nothing executable is affected — but the contradiction is flat and prominent in the two top-level project docs, so MEDIUM stands.

REASONING: Verified directly:
- README.md:10-12 reads "Status: **documentation phase.** The methodology and architecture are being written before any framework code is built." — verbatim as stated.
- docs/CHARTER.md:3 reads "**Status:** Planning / documentation phase." — verbatim as stated.
- src/assay_engine exists with subpackages baseline, contracts, methodology, observability, orchestration, persistence, reasoning, plus registry.py, __init__.py, py.typed; `find ... -name "*.py"` returns 28 modules (matches the "28 modules" claim exactly).
- Test suite: `.venv/bin/python -m pytest -q` → "19 passed in 0.01s" (matches "passing 19-test suite").
- Commit 2c210f9 exists ("feat(engine): scaffold dataset-agnostic engine skeleton + methodology core", 2026-06-15) and its message describes standing up the engine package, contracts, firewalls, and methodology core — directly contradicting "no framework code is built."
- CI present: .github/workflows/ci.yml exists (matches "and CI").

Every factual assertion in the finding is corroborated; I could find nothing to refute it.

FIX_GUIDANCE: Update both status lines to reflect the current state. README.md:10-12 → something like "Status: **engine scaffold in place.** Dataset-agnostic engine (src/assay_engine), methodology core, contracts/firewalls, a passing test suite, and CI are implemented; adapters and studies build on top." docs/CHARTER.md:3 → e.g. "**Status:** Active development — engine skeleton and methodology core implemented (see ARCHITECTURE.md / ADR-0002)." Optionally add a brief note pointing to the test suite and CI as evidence of the current phase, and consider a lightweight check (or a reminder in the hardening workflow) so status lines are revisited when major scaffolding lands.
